title: Unit 13: Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)
description: Find the subset of edges in a weighted graph that connects all vertices with the minimum possible total edge weight.

topics.title: 13.1 Kruskal's Algorithm
topics.description: Sort all edges in increasing order of weight. Pick edges one by one, adding them to the MST if they do not create a cycle (using Union-Find).

complexity.time: O(E log E) or O(E log V) for edge sorting

ispo.desc: Receives edge array list and verticesCount count.
Defines class representation Edge, which stores source, destination, and weights in memory.
Initializes disjoint set parent array, storing self references for all vertices.
Allocates mstEdges count and mstWeight sum trackers in memory.
Allocates rootSrc and rootDest pointers to hold disjoint set representatives.
Sorts edges array in O(E log E) based on edge weights.
Iterates through edges. Performs Union-Find checks to prevent cycles and joins sets if safe.
Performs recursive Disjoint Set parent lookup with path compression.
Outputs the selected MST edge coordinates and corresponding weights directly.

quiz.q: What is the role of the Union-Find (Disjoint Set) data structure in Kruskal's algorithm?
o: To traverse the graph in BFS order
To check if adding an edge will form a cycle in O(1) amortized time

topics.title: 13.2 Prim's Algorithm
topics.description: Start with a single node, and repeatedly add the minimum weight edge that connects a node in the tree to a node outside the tree. Uses a min-priority queue.

complexity.time: O(V^2) or O(E log V) with PriorityQueue

topics.cheatSheet:
Initialize: key[] = infinity, mstSet[] = false. key[0] = 0.
Loop V times. Pick minimum key vertex u not in mstSet. Add u to mstSet. Update keys of adj vertices.

ispo.desc: Accepts adjacency matrix graph representation and vertex count V.
Allocates parent nodes array, key array weights, and mstSet membership boolean array.
Declares u node pointer to hold vertex of minimum key weight outside MST.
Iterates V-1 times. Chooses minimum key node u, sets its visited state, and updates keys of neighboring vertices.
Prints parent-child connections and edge weights of computed MST.

quiz.q: What is the initial key value assigned to all vertices (except source) in Prim's algorithm?

topics.title: 13.3 Total Number of Spanning Trees
topics.description: Calculate total spanning trees of a graph. For complete graphs, use Cayley's formula: V^(V-2). For general graphs, compute the determinant of the Laplacian matrix (Kirchhoff's Matrix Tree Theorem).
topics.cheatSheet:
Kirchhoff Theorem:
1. Construct Laplacian L = Degree - Adjacency.
2. Delete row 1 and column 1.
3. Compute determinant of co-factor matrix.

ispo.desc: Takes number of vertices V.
Declares count variable to hold computed power math result.
Applies Cayley's theorem formula for complete graphs: V to the power of V - 2.
Returns total spanning trees count.

quiz.q: How many spanning trees exist for a complete graph of 4 vertices?

topics.title: 13.4 Minimum Product Spanning Tree
topics.description: Find a spanning tree such that the product of edge weights is minimized. Can be solved by replacing edge weights w with log(w) and running standard MST algorithms (Kruskal/Prim) to minimize the sum of log weights.

complexity.time: O(E log E) or O(E log V)

topics.cheatSheet:
log(w1 * w2 * ... * wN) = log(w1) + log(w2) + ... + log(wN).
Replace edge.weight = Math.log(edge.weight) and run Kruskal's algorithm.

ispo.desc: Accepts edges array and vertex count V.
Transforms edge weight properties in-place by computing their natural logarithms.
Invokes standard Kruskal's MST algorithm on log-transformed weights.

quiz.q: What mathematical identity allows standard sum-minimizing MST algorithms to minimize products?

topics.title: 13.5 Reverse Delete Algorithm for MST
topics.description: Start with the full graph. Sort edges in descending order of weight. Iterate through edges, deleting them unless doing so disconnects the graph (tested using BFS/DFS).
topics.cheatSheet:
Sort edges descending.
For each edge: Delete from graph. Run DFS from source. If visited count < V, add edge back.

ispo.desc: Accepts edges array, vertex count V, and adjacency list graph representation.
Stores current edge reference.
Allocates visited tracker array and connection count accumulator.
Iterates backwards. Disconnects edge, runs DFS to measure connectivity count, and restores edge if count < V.
Prints edges that are retained in the final MST.

quiz.q: In which order does the Reverse Delete algorithm inspect graph edges?

topics.title: 13.6 Cycle Detection in Directed Graph
topics.description: Detect cycles in a directed graph using DFS. Keep track of visited nodes and nodes in the current recursion stack (to identify back edges).

complexity.space: O(V) for arrays and call stack

topics.cheatSheet:
Keep two boolean arrays: visited[] and recStack[].
If neighbor is already in recStack, cycle detected (found a back edge).

ispo.desc: Accepts vertices count V and graph representation adj.
Allocates visited and recStack boolean tracking arrays.
Iterates. Starts DFS recursive check from each unvisited vertex, checking recursion stack intersections.
DFS logic. Sets recStack flag to true, recurses for neighbors, and resets recStack flag to false on backtrack.
Returns true immediately if cycle is detected.
Returns false if all vertices verified cycle-free.

quiz.q: What is the purpose of the 'recStack' (recursion stack) array in directed graph cycle detection?
o: To identify 'back edges' pointing to ancestors currently active in the recursive call path
